title: O365 Multi-Source Failed Authentications Spike
spl:
`o365_management_activity` Workload=AzureActiveDirectory Operation=UserLoginFailed ErrorNumber=50126
      | bucket span=5m _time
      | eval uniqueIPUserCombo = src . "-" . user
      | fillnull
      | stats earliest(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime dc(uniqueIPUserCombo) as uniqueIpUserCombinations, dc(user) as uniqueUsers, dc(src) as uniqueIPs, values(user) as user, values(src) as ips, values(user_agent) as user_agents values(signature) as signature values(src) as src values(dest) as dest
        BY _time vendor_account vendor_product
      | where uniqueIpUserCombinations > 20 AND uniqueUsers > 20 AND uniqueIPs > 20
      | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)`
      | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
      | `o365_multi_source_failed_authentications_spike_filter`
description: The following analytic identifies a spike in failed authentication attempts within an Office 365 environment, indicative of a potential distributed password spraying attack. It leverages UserLoginFailed events from O365 Management Activity logs, focusing on ErrorNumber 50126. This detection is significant as it highlights attempts to bypass security controls using multiple IP addresses and user agents. If confirmed malicious, this activity could lead to unauthorized access, data breaches, privil…
value: The following analytic identifies a spike in failed authentication attempts within an Office 365 environment, indicative of a potential distributed password spraying attack. It leverages UserLoginFailed events from O365 Management Activity logs, focusing on ErrorNumber 50126. implementation: ESCU Hunting detection — not designed for automated alerting. Run ad-hoc or on a low-frequency schedule from Splunk Enterprise Security for analyst-driven investigation. Requires O365 UserLoginFailed ingested and CIM-normalized.
knownFalsePositives: Failed sign-ins and MFA from travelers, new devices, mobile token refresh issues, and passwordless rollout piloting; break-glass and emergency access can also create spikes. Correlate with known user activity.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"O365 Multi-Source Failed Authentications Spike" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: O365 UserLoginFailed. Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1110.003, T1110.004, T1586.003. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "O365 Multi-Source Failed Authentications Spike" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: This detection may yield false positives in scenarios where legitimate bulk sign-in activities occur, such as during company-wide system updates or when users are accessing resources from varying locations in a short time frame, such as in the case of VPNs or cloud services that rotate IP addresses. Filter as needed.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

Validation

Compare results in Splunk with the Microsoft 365 Defender portal (https://security.microsoft.com) for M365- and cloud identity–backed content; use the Proofpoint TAP dashboard for Proofpoint, the Mimecast Personal Portal for Mimecast, and Cisco ESA `mail_logs` and Message Tracking for on-prem gateways.

Confirm the required data sources are flowing:

```spl
| tstats count where index=* by index, sourcetype | sort -count
```

For ESCU correlation searches, verify the search is enabled and recent fires appear as expected:

```spl
index=_audit action="alert_fired" ss_name="*"
| stats count by ss_name, trigger_time | sort -trigger_time
```
grandmaExplanation: We watch Microsoft 365 sign-in and email-related identity signals, so we spot risky logins, token abuse, and account takeover before mailboxes are used to blast attackers’ messages.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable: this use case is not built on a CIM Email `All_Email` summary. Rely on the search in Step 2 (or the ESCU correlation) against your ingested indexes, audit feeds, and vendor consoles.
